text: The surgeon performs a robotic right apical segmentectomy on a sixty-six-year-old female with lung adenocarcinoma. The inferior pulmonary ligament is mobilized up to the inferior pulmonary vein. A level nine lymph node is identified and excised. The lung is retracted medially to reveal the posterior hilum, and the pleura over the subcranial space is mobilized. Level seven lymph nodes are removed for pathological examination. The area is noted to be vascular, requiring careful removal of lymph nodes and achieving hemostasis.
The surgeon dissects around the truncus to identify the apical branch, known as the A1 branch, to the lobe segment. The apical branch is isolated and transected using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon focuses on the apical bronchus segment, carefully avoiding thermal injury as the segment lies adjacent to the pulmonary artery. The lung is folded down to expose the apex, and the apical segment is dissected around. A green loaded stapler is used from the posterior aspect to transect the bronchial segment.

The surgeon performs a test inflation to confirm aeration in the posterior and anterior segments. Firefly imaging is used to demarcate the segmental fissure, followed by marking with bipolar cautery. Multiple green loads of the stapler are used to wedge out the apical segment of the right upper lobe, ensuring the bronchus, vein, and artery are incorporated within the staple line. The lung is flipped back and forth between staple fires to verify the inclusion of these structures.

The surgeon uses black loads of the stapler to transect the thick tissue of the segmental fissure, completing the segmentectomy. A test inflation is performed to confirm that the remaining lung segments inflate properly, indicating successful resection.
